interest: Teachers and their unions seeking to avoid being made the shock absorber for an under-resourced structural reform, joined by families wanting the promised Finnish-style investment actually delivered before their children enter comprehensive classes.
value: Sequencing fairness and adequate working conditions (the burden of reform should not fall on teachers) in tension with the equity ambition of moving fast to abolish early selection.
fear: That the reform's structural change lands years before its enabling investment—retraining, class-size cuts, diagnostic staff—leaving teachers to teach larger, heterogeneous classes unsupported, with reform failure then blamed on 'bad teaching' rather than inadequate resourcing.
affected: pupils in comprehensive classes (both high- and low-achieving)
families of pupils entering the transition cohorts
assumption: That teacher retraining, class-size reductions, and diagnostic/remedial staffing can be delivered fast enough and funded durably enough to arrive before or alongside the comprehensive cohorts, despite a binding teacher shortage and unbounded 'high' costs.
empirical_uncertainty: Contested-to-unknown: the teacher shortage is documented as binding by implementation_planning, but the actual per-cohort/per-district retraining cost has no unit estimate (flagged as understating total exposure), and whether supply/quality upgrades can occur fast enough is explicitly rated 'low confidence.'
